chunk_summary: Strategies for feeding children aged 2-10 with chronic cardiorespiratory disease focus on high-calorie solids, structured meal plans, and school-friendly energy-dense foods to address malnutrition and growth challenges.
learning_objectives: Understand barriers to nutrition in children aged 2-10 with chronic cardiorespiratory disease.
Learn strategies for high-calorie meal planning and snack scaffolding.
Identify school-friendly, energy-dense foods to improve intake during day-care or school routines.
Recognize the impact of infections on appetite and weight in this population.
key_facts: Children aged 2-10 require structured meal plans: 3 meals + 2-3 snacks daily, with optional bedtime or nocturnal supplements for growth faltering.
High-calorie foods include paratha with ghee, curd rice with added oil, enriched khichdi, egg-based dishes, nut pastes, and enriched milk preparations.
Avoid low-calorie displacement foods like large volumes of juice, tea, or health drinks with minimal energy or protein.
During infection seasons, calorie density should be proactively increased to mitigate acute intake reduction and weight loss.
Clinical monitoring includes monthly weight-velocity tracking and escalation to oral nutrition supplements if needed.
reference_quote: Prescribe a minimum scaffolding of three meals plus two to three planned snacks; add a bedtime snack or a nocturnal supplement when growth is faltering.
document_key_topics: High-calorie meal planning for children aged 2-10
Impact of infections on nutrition and growth
